content.text: An Azure resource type defines the schema, API surface, and lifecycle behaviour of a specific kind of Azure resource. Every type belongs to a resource provider namespace (e.g. Microsoft.Compute) and is identified by the pattern Namespace/type — for example, Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines or Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts.

A resource type also carries one or more API versions, each representing a point-in-time snapshot of the resource's REST API. ARM routes each request to the correct API implementation based on the api-version query parameter you supply.

attrs.alt: Resource type hierarchy: provider namespace, resource types, child types, and API version selection

content.text: Resource types follow a two-level path: Namespace/TypeName. Child resource types extend this with additional segments: Namespace/ParentType/ChildType. The full qualified type appears in ARM templates, Bicep declarations, resource IDs, and the az resource CLI.

Resource types are case-insensitive. Microsoft.Compute/VirtualMachines and microsoft.compute/virtualmachines refer to the same type. The Azure portal and CLI return types in a consistent mixed-case form.

# List all resource types in a provider namespace
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.Compute \
  --query 'resourceTypes[].{type:resourceType, apiVersions:apiVersions[0]}' \
  -o table

# Find a specific resource type and its supported API versions
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.Compute \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='virtualMachines'].{type:resourceType, versions:apiVersions}" \
  -o json

# List all resource types across ALL registered providers in the subscription
az resource list \
  --query '[].type' \
  -o tsv | sort -u
Listing Available Types for a Provider

# List resource types registered in your subscription
az provider list --query '[?registrationState==`Registered`].namespace' -o tsv

# Get all resource types for a provider including locations and capabilities
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.Network \
  --query 'resourceTypes[].{type:resourceType, locations:locations[0:3], zones:zoneMappings}' \
  -o table

# Check if a specific resource type supports a specific API version
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.Storage \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='storageAccounts'].apiVersions" \
  -o json

Every resource type operation requires an api-version query parameter. ARM uses this to select the correct schema and feature set. Using a deprecated API version may cause failures or missing properties. Using a preview version gives early access to features that are not yet guaranteed stable.

# The API version controls which properties and features are available.
# Always use the latest stable version unless you need a specific preview feature.

# Get the latest stable API version for a resource type
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.Compute \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='virtualMachines'].apiVersions[0]" \
  -o tsv

# REST API call with explicit API version
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myapp-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myvm?api-version=2023-03-01'

# Preview API versions end in '-preview'
# Example: 2023-09-01-preview has new features not in 2023-03-01
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myapp-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myvm?api-version=2023-09-01-preview'
Testing new features not in stable
In Bicep, the resource type and API version appear together in the resource declaration string. Child resources reference their parent using the parent keyword.

Terraform's azurerm provider maps Azure resource types to provider-managed resource blocks. For preview API features not yet supported by azurerm, use the azapi provider to call the ARM REST API directly.
Querying Resource Types with the Python SDK

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ResourceManagementClient(credential, subscription_id)

# List all resource types for a provider namespace
provider = client.providers.get('Microsoft.Compute')
for rt in provider.resource_types:
    latest = rt.api_versions[0] if rt.api_versions else 'N/A'
    print(f'{rt.resource_type}: latest={latest}')

# Find which API versions are available for a specific type
compute = client.providers.get('Microsoft.Compute')
vm_type = next(
    rt for rt in compute.resource_types
    if rt.resource_type == 'virtualMachines'
)
print('Supported API versions:', vm_type.api_versions)
print('Default API version:', vm_type.default_api_version)

A child resource type exists only within the context of a parent resource. Its ID appends the child type and name to the parent's ID. Common examples include VM extensions, storage containers, SQL databases (under SQL Server), and VNet subnets.

# Child resources are nested under a parent resource type
# Full type: Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/extensions
# Full ID: .../virtualMachines/myvm/extensions/AzureMonitorLinuxAgent

# List child resources (extensions) under a VM
az vm extension list \
  --vm-name myvm \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --query '[].{name:name, type:typePropertiesType, state:provisioningState}' \
  -o table

# List all subnets (child of virtualNetworks)
az network vnet subnet list \
  --vnet-name myvnet \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --query '[].{name:name, prefix:addressPrefix, nsg:networkSecurityGroup.id}' \
  -o table

# Get a child resource by its full type path
az resource show \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/extensions \
  --parent virtualMachines/myvm \
  --name AzureMonitorLinuxAgent

Each resource type exposes metadata about which Azure regions it can deploy to, which availability zones it supports, and what capabilities it offers. Query this metadata from the provider API to validate region selection before deployment.

# Resource types expose different capabilities — query them from the provider API

# 1. Supported locations for a resource type
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.Compute \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='virtualMachines'].locations" \
  -o tsv

# 2. Zone mappings (which zones are available per region)
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.Compute \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='virtualMachines'].zoneMappings[?location=='East US 2']"

# 3. Capabilities (e.g. 'SupportsAvailabilityZone', 'SupportedByManagedIdentity')
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.Compute \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='virtualMachines'].capabilities"

# 4. Operations exposed by the resource type (for RBAC definition)
az provider operation list \
  --query "[?name=='Microsoft.Compute'].resourceTypes[?name=='virtualMachines'].operations[].name" \
  -o tsv

Azure Policy conditions match on resource type using the 'type' field. This lets you write policies that apply to specific resource types — or specific child resource types — across your subscriptions.

In ARM JSON templates, the resource type appears in a 'type' field alongside an 'apiVersion'. Child resource names include the parent name as a prefix, joined by '/', to express the full resource path.

Not all Azure resource types are created as children of a parent or as standalone resources. Extension resource types attach to an existing resource as an overlay — they extend the behavior of a target resource without being a child of it. Role assignments, locks, diagnostic settings, and tags are extension resources.

# Extension resource types extend OTHER resources without being children of them.
# They are deployed 'on top of' a resource using the resource as their scope.

# Common extension resource types:
#   Microsoft.Authorization/locks         — resource locks
#   Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments — RBAC assignments
#   Microsoft.Insights/diagnosticSettings — diagnostic settings
#   Microsoft.Resources/tags             — tag operations

# Deploy a lock as an extension resource on a storage account
az lock create \
  --name mylock \
  --lock-type CanNotDelete \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --resource-name mystorageacct \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts

# In Bicep, extension resources use the 'scope' property:
# resource lock 'Microsoft.Authorization/locks@2020-05-01' = {
#   name: 'mylock'
#   scope: storageAccount  // extends the storage account, not a child of it
#   properties: { level: 'CanNotDelete' }
# }

ARM resource types support different operation patterns. Simple resources (NSGs, VNets) return synchronously. Larger resources (VMs, AKS) return 202 and require polling. Some types also expose non-CRUD 'action' operations via HTTP POST.

# Different resource types support different operation modes

# 1. Synchronous PUT (returns 200/201 when done)
#    Most simple resources: storage accounts, VNets, NSGs
az network nsg create --name mynsg --resource-group myapp-rg
# Returns immediately with the created NSG

# 2. Asynchronous PUT (returns 201/202 + Location header)
#    Longer operations: VMs, AKS clusters, SQL databases
az vm create \
  --name myvm \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --admin-username azureuser
# CLI polls the async operation until completion

# 3. Action operations (not PUT/GET/DELETE — invoked via POST)
#    e.g. start/stop/restart VM, regenerate storage key
az vm start --name myvm --resource-group myapp-rg
az storage account keys renew \
  --account-name mystorageacct \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --key primary
Commonly Used Resource Types — Quick Reference
Resource Types at Scale — Azure Resource Graph
Azure Resource Graph indexes all resources across your subscriptions and lets you query resource types, counts, and properties at scale using KQL.

# Azure Resource Graph: query resource types at scale across subscriptions

# Count resources by type across all subscriptions
az graph query -q "
Resources
| summarize count() by type
| order by count_ desc
| limit 20
" -o table

# Find all resource types in use in a specific resource group
az graph query -q "
Resources
| where resourceGroup == 'myapp-rg'
| distinct type
| order by type
" -o table

# Find resources that are an uncommon/non-Microsoft type (third-party)
az graph query -q "
Resources
| where type !startswith 'microsoft.'
| project type, name, resourceGroup
"

Each resource type exposes metadata about which Azure regions it can deploy to, which availability zones it supports, and what capabilities it offers. Query this metadata from the provider API to validate region selection before deployment.

# Resource types expose different capabilities — query them from the provider API

# 1. Supported locations for a resource type
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.Compute \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='virtualMachines'].locations" \
  -o tsv

# 2. Zone mappings (which zones are available per region)
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.Compute \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='virtualMachines'].zoneMappings[?location=='East US 2']"

# 3. Capabilities (e.g. 'SupportsAvailabilityZone', 'SupportedByManagedIdentity')
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.Compute \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='virtualMachines'].capabilities"

# 4. Operations exposed by the resource type (for RBAC definition)
az provider operation list \
  --query "[?name=='Microsoft.Compute'].resourceTypes[?name=='virtualMachines'].operations[].name" \
  -o tsv

# }

Resource Types at Scale — Azure Resource Graph
Azure Resource Graph indexes all resources across your subscriptions and lets you query resource types, counts, and properties at scale using KQL.

" -o table

| where resourceGroup == 'myapp-rg'

Microsoft periodically deprecates old API versions. Deployments using deprecated API versions may receive warnings or eventually fail. Keep IaC files current by checking for outdated API version strings regularly.

# Detect deprecated API versions in your Bicep/ARM templates
# ARM will warn (or block) calls to deprecated api-versions

# Check which api-versions are marked 'deprecated' in provider metadata
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.Compute \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='virtualMachines'].apiVersions" \
  -o json
# Note: Azure does not expose deprecation status via the provider API directly;
# check the ARM REST API reference docs for each type.

# Lint Bicep files for outdated API versions
az bicep build --file main.bicep  # compiler warns on very old versions

# Use the Azure Resource Manager Tools VS Code extension:
# - Highlights api-versions older than 2 years
# - Suggests the latest stable alternative

# In Terraform, upgrade the azurerm provider version to get newer API versions:
# terraform init -upgrade
# terraform providers

Many resource types support multiple SKUs (Stock Keeping Units) that determine pricing tier, throughput, redundancy, and feature availability. The SKU is part of the resource's definition, not a separate resource type.

# Some resource types have SKUs that control pricing tier and capabilities

# List available SKUs for Virtual Machines in a region
az vm list-skus \
  --location eastus2 \
  --resource-type virtualMachines \
  --query '[].{name:name, family:family, vCPUs:capabilities[?name==`vCPUs`].value | [0]}' \
  -o table | head -20

# List Storage Account SKUs
az storage account list-skus \
  --location eastus2 \
  --query '[].{name:name, tier:tier, kind:kind}' \
  -o table

# List App Service Plan SKUs
az appservice list-locations \
  --sku S1 S2 S3 P1v3 P2v3 P3v3 \
  --query '[].name' -o tsv
vCPU count, RAM, temp disk, network bandwidth
CPU, RAM, scaling, custom domains, slots

Azure resource types follow the pattern Namespace/TypeName (e.g. Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines). Each type exposes one or more API versions; always use the latest stable version in production. Child resource types extend a parent type with additional path segments. Use az provider show to discover available types and API versions for any registered provider namespace.
